children.value: The balanced lifestyle perspective focuses on combining studying with leisure activities. In this section, we examine how alcohol consumption and gaming affect academic performance.

The first argument supports the perspective that having a balanced lifestyle can improve students’ academic performance by reducing harmful behaviors such as excessive alcohol consumption. The article from
shows that higher alcohol consumption is associated with lower student achievement, especially among high-performing students.

children.value: Figure 6: Final grade by average alcohol consumption

The two visualizations show a weak negative correlation between final grades, average alcohol consumption, and Math/Portuguese. Across both figures, a slight decline in performance can be observed as alcohol consumption increases, with the effect appearing somewhat stronger for Portuguese than for Mathematics. In addition, figure 5 suggests that alcohol consumption levels between 4 and 5 are underrepresented, which may have influenced these results. Overall, our findings partially support
, which also found that higher alcohol consumption was associated with lower grades, although the relationship in our data appears less pronounced.

The second argument supporting the balanced lifestyle perspective is that increased time spent gaming may negatively affect grades, but can simultaneously enhance response times, which may in turn improve educational performance. This claim is supported by research from
, which found that faster response times are associated with improved cognitive performance. Additionally,

demonstrates that gaming can significantly improve response times. Taken together, these findings suggest that gaming may indirectly support certain aspects of academic performance through cognitive enhancement, although excessive gaming may have negative effects on grades.
Figure 7: Reaction time vs Grades vs Gaming Hours
Figure 8: How reaction time influences grades

The two figures suggest that reaction time does not strongly influence grades, as the data shows no clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, time spent gaming appears more related to grades, with a more noticeable trend. Combined, these results do not support reaction time as an important predictor of academic performance in this dataset. This is interesting because it contrasts with findings from

, which report a positive relationship. The study suggests that faster reaction times may indicate quicker information processing, better attentional control, and more efficient cognitive functioning, all of which can support learning and test performance.

Carrell_2011.label: Carrell_2011
Carrell_2011.html: Carrell, S. E., Hoekstra, M., & West, J. E. (2011). Does drinking impair college performance? Evidence from a regression discontinuity approach. Journal of Public Economics, 95(1–2), 54–62. 10.1016/j.jpubeco.2010.08.008

How does cognitive function measured by the reaction time and critical flicker fusion frequency correlate with the academic performance of students? BMC Medical Education, 20(1). 10.1186/s12909-020-02416-7

Chandra_2016.html: Chandra, S., Sharma, G., Salam, A. A., Jha, D., & Mittal, A. P. (2016). Playing Action Video Games a Key to Cognitive Enhancement. Procedia Computer Science, 84, 115–122. 10.1016/j.procs.2016.04.074
